description: The oceanic whitetip shark (Carcharhinus longimanus) is a large requiem shark inhabiting the pelagic zone of tropical and warm temperate seas. It has a stocky body and iconic elongated rounded fins, with white tips. The species is typically solitary, though they may gather in large numbers at food concentrations.
funFact: Once considered one of the most abundant large ocean animals on Earth, the Oceanic Whitetip Shark has suffered a population collapse estimated at over 98% in parts of the Atlantic since the 1950s — one of the most dramatic declines of any large marine predator ever recorded.
habitat: Found worldwide between 45°N and 43°S latitude in deep, open ocean. Prefers water temperatures above 20°C (68°F) but can range into waters as cool as 15°C (59°F). A truly pelagic shark, it rarely approaches coastlines or shallow areas.
diet: An opportunistic apex predator feeding primarily on bony fish, cephalopods (squid and octopus), and occasionally sea turtles, seabirds, and marine mammals. Known to follow pods of pilot whales and schools of tuna across the open ocean.
size: Typically 1.8–2.9 m (6–9.5 ft); max recorded 3.9 m (12.8 ft). Females are larger than males.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: Severely depleted — no reliable global estimate

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Bony fish, squid, sea turtles, seabirds, marine mammals

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Very low — late maturity and small litter sizes

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Highly attuned olfaction; broadly rounded pectoral fins optimized for open-ocean cruising; often followed by pilot fish associations

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Worldwide in tropical and warm temperate open ocean, 45°N to 43°S

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: Surface to ~1,082 m (3,550 ft); primarily epipelagic

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: The primary driver of decline. Caught in enormous numbers in pelagic longlines targeting tuna and swordfish. Once the most common bycatch shark in these fisheries, it is now rarely encountered.

threats.name: Shark Fin Trade
threats.description: Heavily targeted for the fin trade due to its large, elongated pectoral fins. Finning at sea was common before international restrictions, but illegal finning continues in unmonitored high-seas fisheries.

threats.name: Inadequate Fisheries Management
threats.description: Despite CITES Appendix II listing and catch limits in some RFMOs, enforcement is weak and illegal, unreported, and unregulated (IUU) fishing remains widespread across pelagic waters.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: Warming oceans and shifts in tuna distributions alter prey availability and may push the species into higher-pressure fishing zones closer to port fisheries.

actionItems.title: Support Pelagic Shark Protection
actionItems.description: Advocate for stronger RFMO catch limits and better enforcement of CITES Appendix II protections for oceanic whitetips across international waters.

actionItems.title: Choose Sustainable Seafood
actionItems.description: Use the Monterey Bay Aquarium Seafood Watch guide to avoid tuna and swordfish from fisheries with high bycatch rates where oceanic whitetips are common.

actionItems.title: Refuse Shark Fin Products
actionItems.description: Avoid purchasing or consuming shark fin soup and products, and support businesses that have pledged to remove them from their menus.

actionItems.title: Donate to Open-Ocean Conservation
actionItems.description: Support organizations like the Shark Trust and Save Our Seas Foundation that fund research and advocacy for pelagic shark species.

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Declining — prey loss and warming in pelagic zones

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining — tuna and billfish heavily depleted

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: Critical — pelagic longlines span the tropics

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Limited — CITES II; RFMO limits poorly enforced

populationTrendMeta.note: Rough global estimates modeled from documented regional declines, including >98% depletion in the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic since the 1950s. Historical abundance may have reached into the millions; absolute global counts have never been conducted.

preyDeclineRegions.name: N. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Tuna and swordfish heavily depleted since the 1950s

preyDeclineRegions.name: Gulf of Mexico
preyDeclineRegions.note: Tuna and billfish collapsed; whitetips nearly gone

preyDeclineRegions.name: Mediterranean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Bluefin near collapse; squid and billfish depleted

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Pelagic tuna under heavy longline and purse seine pressure

preyDeclineRegions.name: S. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Tuna and squid under rising distant-water fleet pressure

preyDeclineRegions.name: N. Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Albacore and skipjack heavily fished; partial recovery underway

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Dense pelagic longlines; severe historic and ongoing bycatch

fishingPressureRegions.name: Gulf of Mexico
fishingPressureRegions.note: Epicenter of >98% collapse; poor observer coverage

fishingPressureRegions.name: Mediterranean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Dense longlines and drift nets; minimal enforcement

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Heavy longline effort; CITES poorly enforced offshore

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: WCPFC longlines active; observer coverage improving
